perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such material, and shall certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall make a good-faith effort to deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without explicitly retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such material, and may certify the destruction of such materials to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly deliver' to 'shall make a good-faith effort' weakens the obligation to return or destroy materials. Changing 'without retaining' to 'without explicitly retaining' creates a loophole for implicit retention. Changing 'shall certify' to 'may certify' makes certification optional.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Penal Law § 175.00 defines 'business records' and relates to offenses involving false written statements. By weakening the requirement to destroy and certify the destruction of documents, it creates an opportunity for falsification or concealment of business records relevant to the possible transaction, especially given the 'good-faith effort' and 'without explicitly retaining' changes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that directly undermine the original contract's obligations regarding the handling of evaluation materials. Specifically, changing 'shall promptly deliver' to 'shall make a good-faith effort' weakens the obligation to return or destroy materials, and changing 'shall certify' to 'may certify' makes certification optional. According to the Law Explanation, this creates opportunities for falsification or concealment. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'business record' as 'any writing or article... kept or maintained by an enterprise for the purpose of evidencing or reflecting its condition or activity.' The original contract ensures proper handling and documentation of these records, while the changed text weakens these requirements, creating a contradiction because the contract now allows for less reliable handling of business records.

perturbation.original_text: You also agree not to discuss with or offer to any third party an equity participation in a Possible Transaction or any other form of joint acquisition by you and such third party without CSFB's prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree to generally avoid discussing with or offering to any third party an equity participation in a Possible Transaction or any other form of joint acquisition by you and such third party without considering CSFB's opinion.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agree not to discuss' with 'agree to generally avoid discussing' softens the prohibition. Replacing 'prior written consent' with 'considering CSFB's opinion' makes the requirement subjective and non-binding. This makes it less clear what's expected and enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. Secret agreements to divide markets or fix prices would violate this Act. Weakening the prohibition on discussing joint acquisitions creates an environment where illegal coordination is more likely. Since the consent is now 'considering CSFB's opinion', agreements could be made without formal documentation or consent, potentially violating the Act if it leads to anti-competitive behavior.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 1 / § 1 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 1 : Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 1-MONOPOLIES AND COMBINATIONS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date Short Title Executive Documents §1. Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the prohibition against discussing joint acquisitions and replaces the requirement for 'prior written consent' with 'considering CSFB's opinion,' making the obligation subjective. The law snippet 1 (15 USC 1) states that 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal.' By making the prohibition less stringent, the changed text potentially facilitates agreements that could restrain trade, thus creating a contradiction by increasing the risk of violating the Sherman Act. The original text's clear prohibition reduces this risk, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and discretion that could be used to circumvent the law's intent.
